title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Stephen Megalas identifies Wileg Croce as the individual responsible for leaving the "T" symbol, which Megalas interprets as a symbol of life. Following a summary by Detective Vance regarding the murders of Andrew Van and Thomas Braid—including the discovery of the school principal's body and testimony from a garage owner named Crocker—Megalas insists on being taken to the gazebo.

At the gazebo, Megalas ignores the bloodstains and the T-shaped symbol to focus on a carved wooden pipe shaped like a sea god's head and trident. Although investigators previously believed the pipe belonged to Braid, based on the testimony of a man named Stollings and the presence of Braid's fingerprints and tobacco, Megalas reveals the pipe is actually his own, lost a year prior. Megalas notes that Braid would never have smoked another person's pipe due to an aversion to dirt.

Ellery Queen proposes a hypothesis to explain this discrepancy. He notes that Megalas has an impeccable alibi, as Captain Swift provides logbook entries and a Panama Canal receipt proving the yacht *Helen* was in the West Indies and the Pacific on June 22nd. Ellery argues that the murderer, Croce, used Megalas's pipe to stage the scene. By loading and smoking the pipe, the killer attempted to create the false impression that Braid was alive and walking toward the pavilion in a smoking suit before his death. Ellery concludes that Croce obtained the pipe from a desk drawer in the study.
